name: Rainforest rain on big-leaf maple
location: Hoh River valley, Olympic National Park, Olympic Peninsula, Washington

species.note: Largest maple species in North America. Native Pacific Coast Alaska to California. Largest leaves of any N. American deciduous tree: 15-30 cm across.

precipitation.status: Wettest place in continental US (outside highest mountain ranges)
precipitation.mechanism: Pacific storms + Olympic Mountains orographic lift
precipitation.rain_shadow_comparison: Sequim, WA (opposite slope): 16 in/year -- less than Los Angeles

acoustic_layers.name: High canopy impacts
acoustic_layers.description: Fine rain on topmost leaves. Soft, filtered patter from above.

acoustic_layers.name: Mid-canopy drips
acoustic_layers.description: Coalesced drops falling to mid-canopy. Larger, more percussive, irregular intervals.

acoustic_layers.name: Understory complexity
acoustic_layers.description: Direct rain + canopy drip + moss-saturated release. Most complex acoustic layer.

acoustic_layers.name: Ground plops
acoustic_layers.description: Large drips on saturated soil, standing water, ferns, fallen logs. Softest, deepest.

leaf_physics.impact_sound: Depends on drop mass, velocity, leaf size and thickness
leaf_physics.resonant_frequency: Proportional to leaf size: large leaves = low pitch; small leaves = high pitch
leaf_physics.drip_cascade: Single drop initiates cascade: leaf 1 → drip → leaf 2 → drip → ground. Delay chain extends acoustic consequence.
leaf_physics.moss_factor: Moss absorbs impact sound; releases water as slow, irregular drips when saturated

hoh_ecosystem.river: Hoh River (56 miles; rises from Mount Olympus glaciers; Pacific Ocean outlet)
hoh_ecosystem.mount_olympus_elevation: 7980 feet (2432 m); most glaciated peak in contiguous US outside Alaska
hoh_ecosystem.protection: Olympic National Park (1938); UNESCO World Heritage Site; Biosphere Reserve

hoh_tribe.territory: Lower Hoh River and adjacent coastline
hoh_tribe.significance: Subsistence, cultural, spiritual practices centered on Hoh River and rainforest

theory_nodes.concept: FIRST METEOROLOGY ENTRY: the abiotic era's second family opens. Hydrology (245-247) = water in the landscape. Meteorology (248+) = water in the atmosphere. Rain is the link between families: precipitation feeds rivers. The classification system expands to match the subject matter.

Big-leaf maple as percussion instrument: leaf size determines resonant frequency (large = bass, small = treble). Multiple leaf sizes = continuous pitch gradient. Multiple acoustic layers (canopy to ground). Rain on maple = natural drum kit with stochastic rhythm and gradient pitch range.
theory_nodes.description: The acoustic mechanics of rainfall on maple leaves.

theory_nodes.concept: Water arc 245-248: river current (distributed/polyrhythm) → Snoqualmie Falls (concentrated/white-noise) → Sol Duc Falls (enclosed/resonant) → Hoh rain (atmospheric/stochastic). Four modes of the same element. The arc describes the water cycle: precipitation → river → waterfall → back to precipitation.
theory_nodes.description: The four-entry water arc as a complete hydrological cycle.

cross_references.relationship: Previous Olympic Peninsula entry. Falls (continuous, high-energy roar) vs. rain (stochastic, low-energy patter). Same ecosystem region; different meteorological/hydrological mode.
Chinook salmon run up Hoh River -- all five salmon species in Hoh River, one of few remaining. Cross-reference: biotic life sustained by the abiotic precipitation documented here.
Dipper sings next to streams. Both the dipper's stream habitat and the Hoh rainforest rain are aspects of the same water-dominated PNW acoustic environment.

s36_pair.pairing_note: E=4 + E=7 = E=11. Most organized human percussion (Thunderpussy drums: structured, rhythmically precise, intentional) vs. most random natural percussion (rain on maple: stochastic, no rhythm, governed by physics). Both percussion-forward. The contrast is the content: human intention vs. natural process.
